one_liner: A 26-year-old woman presents with a 10-hour history of severe right-sided throbbing headache, nausea, and intolerance of bright light and loud sound.

stage2.hpi_extension: She describes a **unilateral right-sided pulsating headache** that began gradually and has worsened over the past several hours. She has had similar episodes every 1-2 months for the past 3 years, each lasting anywhere from 6 to 48 hours. About 20 minutes before the pain started, she saw **shimmering zig-zag lines in her right visual field (scintillating scotoma)** that slowly faded. She is now lying in a dark, quiet room because **bright light and loud sound make it worse (photophobia and phonophobia)**. She has vomited twice. Attacks are often triggered by **stress, menstruation, sleep deprivation, red wine, and aged cheese**.
stage2.medications: Combined oral contraceptive pill, ibuprofen as needed (minimal relief)
stage2.family_history: Mother and older sister both have **recurrent disabling headaches**
stage2.social_history: Graduate student. No tobacco, occasional wine. Sleeps 5-6 hours nightly during exams.
stage2.physical_exam: Appears uncomfortable, lying with eyes closed and a pillow over her head. Neuro: cranial nerves II-XII intact, no focal weakness, normal sensation, reflexes symmetric, no cerebellar or meningeal signs. Funduscopy normal (no papilledema). Neck supple. No scalp or temporal artery tenderness.

lab_results.result: MRI brain with and without contrast: **no acute infarct, hemorrhage, mass, or vascular malformation**. No evidence of hydrocephalus or midline shift. Normal study.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Imaging is typically normal in migraine. It is performed mainly to rule out secondary causes (e.g., tumor, hemorrhage, vascular malformation) when headaches are new, severe, or have red-flag features.

lab_results.result: Sodium 140, potassium 4.1, glucose 92, creatinine 0.7, LFTs normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Normal labs argue against metabolic or toxic causes of headache.

stage5.final_clue: She is given IV metoclopramide and a **subcutaneous sumatriptan (5-HT1B/1D agonist)**, and within 45 minutes her pain and nausea resolve. A propranolol prophylaxis plan is started after a discussion of trigger avoidance and sleep hygiene.

debrief.classic_presentation: Migraine classically presents in young to middle-aged women as episodic **unilateral pulsating/throbbing headaches** lasting 4 to 72 hours, accompanied by **nausea, vomiting, photophobia, and phonophobia**. About one third of patients experience a preceding **aura** — most commonly visual (scintillating scotomas, zig-zag lines, fortification spectra), and less commonly sensory or language disturbance — from cortical spreading depression. Triggers include **stress, menstruation, certain foods (red wine, aged cheese, chocolate), sleep deprivation, and oral contraceptives**. POUND mnemonic: Pulsatile, One-day duration (4-72 h), Unilateral, Nausea, Disabling.
debrief.pathophysiology: Migraine is a primary neurovascular headache driven by activation of the **trigeminovascular system**. Release of vasoactive neuropeptides — most importantly **calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP)**, substance P, and neurokinin A — causes neurogenic inflammation and sensitization of trigeminal afferents around meningeal vessels. The aura corresponds to a wave of **cortical spreading depression** (slowly propagating neuronal depolarization followed by suppression). Serotonergic pathways are also involved, which is why 5-HT1B/1D agonists (triptans) abort attacks.
debrief.treatment_overview: Acute (abortive) therapy: NSAIDs and acetaminophen for mild attacks; **triptans (sumatriptan)** — 5-HT1B/1D receptor agonists that cause intracranial vasoconstriction and block CGRP release — for moderate-severe attacks; antiemetics such as metoclopramide or prochlorperazine; ergotamine/DHE as alternatives. Triptans and ergots are **contraindicated in coronary artery disease, uncontrolled hypertension, and Prinzmetal angina** because of vasoconstriction. Prophylaxis (if more than 4 headaches/month or debilitating): **beta-blockers (propranolol, metoprolol)**, topiramate, valproate, TCAs (amitriptyline), and **CGRP antagonists (erenumab)**. Lifestyle: regular sleep, trigger avoidance, hydration.
high_yield_pearls: POUND criteria for migraine: Pulsatile, One-day duration, Unilateral, Nausea, Disabling.
Aura = cortical spreading depression. Most auras are visual (scintillating scotomas) and last <60 minutes before the headache.
CGRP release in the trigeminovascular system drives migraine pain — targeted by erenumab (anti-CGRP monoclonal antibody).
Triptans (sumatriptan) are 5-HT1B/1D agonists; avoid in CAD, uncontrolled HTN, and Prinzmetal angina due to vasoconstriction.
Migraine with aura plus combined OCP plus smoking substantially raises ischemic stroke risk — switch to progestin-only contraception.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Neurology and Special Senses chapter, 'Headaches — Migraine' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Neurology and Special Senses chapter, 'Migraine drugs — Triptans' section.
